is a type of [sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport") where the fundamental nature of the game or sport requires the participation of multiple individuals working together as a [team](/wiki/Team "Team"), and it is inherently impossible or highly impractical to execute the sport as a single-player endeavour. In team sports, the cooperative effort of [team](/wiki/Team "Team") members is essential for the sport to function and achieve its objectives. The objective often involves teammates facilitating the movement of a ball or similar bob in accordance with a set of rules in order to score points. Examples are [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball"), [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"), [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"), [water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo"), [handball](/wiki/Handball "Handball"), [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse"), [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball"), and the various forms of [football](/wiki/Football "Football") and [hockey](/wiki/Hockey "Hockey"). These sports emphasize teamwork, strategy, and coordination among team members while competing against opposing teams to achieve a common goal.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Team sports do not include individual or individual-to-team events within a sport.

A **team sport** is a type of [sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport") where the fundamental nature of the game or sport requires the participation of multiple individuals working together as a [team](/wiki/Team "Team"), and it is inherently impossible or highly impractical to execute the sport as a single-player endeavour. In team sports, the cooperative effort of [team](/wiki/Team "Team") members is essential for the sport to function and achieve its objectives. The objective often involves teammates facilitating the movement of a ball or similar bob in accordance with a set of rules in order to score points. Examples are [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball"), [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"), [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"), [water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo"), [handball](/wiki/Handball "Handball"), [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse"), [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball"), and the various forms of [football](/wiki/Football "Football") and [hockey](/wiki/Hockey "Hockey"). These sports emphasize teamwork, strategy, and coordination among team members while competing against opposing teams to achieve a common goal.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Team sports do not include individual or individual-to-team events within a sport.

## Distinctions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Team_sport&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Distinctions")]

The meaning of a "team sport" has been disputed in recent years. Some types of sports have different objectives or rules than "traditional" team sports. These types of team sports do not involve teammates facilitating the movement of a ball or similar object in accordance with a set of rules in order to score points. Overall, the division into team sports and individual sports is not always unproblematic since there are different combinations in the individual sports.

### Team sports and individual-to-team events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Team_sport&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Team sports and individual-to-team events")]

#### Pure team sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Team_sport&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pure team sports")]

This includes sports that can only be practiced as a team sport. The number of team members is fixed for the team. In order to compete successfully in championships and tournaments, teams need a roster that is significantly larger than the number of players starting the game. Players may be substituted from a squad in competition matches to replace exhausted or injured players or to make tactical changes. Examples are [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball"), [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"), [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"), [water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo"), [handball](/wiki/Handball "Handball"), [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse"), [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball"), and the various forms of [football](/wiki/Football "Football") and [hockey](/wiki/Hockey "Hockey").

Teams of two people are common in certain sports, such as [dancesport](/wiki/Dancesport "Dancesport") or [beach volleyball](/wiki/Beach_volleyball "Beach volleyball"). No substitute players are used here. The two partners are absolutely dependent on each other. The absence of a person here means a loss of competitiveness.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

#### Formations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Team_sport&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Formations")]

There are sports that are commonly played by individuals, or singles, but may also be played in a cooperative partner formation referred to as doubles. This includes sports such as [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton"), [table tennis](/wiki/Table_tennis "Table tennis"), and [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis").[[5]](#cite_note-5) In these formations, common tactics, teamwork, and agreements are crucial for success. There are also different [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)") formations, such as one, two, four, and eight, or [sailing](/wiki/Sailing_(sport) "Sailing (sport)") with their different boat classes.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The most important thing here is smooth movements and common tactics. This also applies approximately to the [team time trial](/wiki/Team_time_trial "Team time trial") in [cycling](/wiki/Cycling "Cycling"),[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) which is the case with cycling tours and one-day races, which are different tasks for the team members of a cycling team.[[10]](#cite_note-cycling_roles-10)[[11]](#cite_note-cycling_captain-11)

#### Relays

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Team_sport&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Relays")]

In some sports, [relay races](/wiki/Relay_race "Relay race") are held, which can be distinguished from pure team evaluations by a common racing tactic and the observance of change regulations. Relay races are common in [running](/wiki/Running "Running"), [swimming](/wiki/Swimming "Swimming"), [cross-country skiing](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing "Cross-country skiing"), [biathlon](/wiki/Biathlon "Biathlon"), or [short-track speed skating](/wiki/Short-track_speed_skating "Short-track speed skating") and are also an integral part of the [Olympic Games program](/wiki/Olympic_sports "Olympic sports")[[12]](#cite_note-12) with high popularity.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

#### Team ratings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Team_sport&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Team ratings")]

There are team ratings in many sports, and the results of individual athletes or formations are added up. In [cycling](/wiki/Cycling "Cycling"), team members, whilst still in competition with each other, will also work towards assisting one member of the team, usually a specialist, to the highest possible finishing position.[[11]](#cite_note-cycling_captain-11)[[10]](#cite_note-cycling_roles-10)[[15]](#cite_note-tdf2011-15) In some sports where participants are entered by a team, they do not only compete against members of other teams, but also against each other for points towards championship standings, for example, in motorsport, particularly [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One").[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Team orders](/wiki/Team_orders "Team orders") can occur in such teams and although previously accepted were banned in Formula One[[17]](#cite_note-17) between 2002 and 2010. After a controversy involving [team orders at the 2010 German Grand Prix](/wiki/Team_orders_at_the_2010_German_Grand_Prix "Team orders at the 2010 German Grand Prix") however, the regulation was removed as of the [2011](/wiki/2011_Formula_One_World_Championship "2011 Formula One World Championship") season.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Overview comparison

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Team_sport&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Overview comparison")]

Overview comparison

| Team sports | Formations in sports | Relay races | Sports with team ratings |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| (e.g., Soccer, Basketball) | (e.g., Synchronized Swimming, Doubles Tennis) | (e.g., Track and Field relay, Swimming relay) | (e.g., Gymnastics, Figure skating) |
| * In team sports, the entire game or match revolves around team dynamics. * The outcome depends on collective performance throughout the entire duration of the match. * The teamwork, strategy, and coordination among team members are crucial for success. * Do not include individual or individual-to-team events within a sport. | * These sports can be performed individually, but they also have team variations. * In synchronized swimming, swimmers perform coordinated routines together. * In doubles tennis, two players collaborate as a team to compete against another pair. * Team formations enhance the strategic and cooperative elements of these sports. | * Relay races involve teams, but they are often a series of individual efforts within a team context. * Each team member runs a segment of the race independently. * Success depends on both individual speed and the smooth transition between the athletes. | * These sports include individual performances, but the team rating considers the cumulative scores of all team members. * Team members' performances contribute to the team's overall score, but they may perform their routines individually. * Team strategy involves selecting the right competitors for specific events to maximize the team's chances. |

In summary, team sports are characterized by the impossibility or impracticality of executing the sport as a single-player endeavor, and the entire game or match relies on team dynamics. In contrast, sports with team ratings and formations involve both individual and team aspects, where individual performances contribute to a team's overall success but may not be entirely reliant on team dynamics. Relay races combine individual efforts within a team context, where smooth transitions are essential.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Team_sport&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of sport](/wiki/History_of_sport "History of sport")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Pankratiasten_in_fight_copy_of_greek_statue_3_century_bC.jpg/250px-Pankratiasten_in_fight_copy_of_greek_statue_3_century_bC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pankratiasten_in_fight_copy_of_greek_statue_3_century_bC.jpg)

Ancient Greek wrestlers

Areas around the Mediterranean had a long tradition of athletic events. Ancient Egyptians and Mesopotamians depicted athletic scenes in the tombs of kings and their nobles. They did not, however, hold regular competitions, and those events that occurred were probably the preserve of kings and upper classes. Minoans culture held gymnastics in high esteem, with [bull-leaping](/wiki/Bull-leaping "Bull-leaping"), tumbling, running, wrestling and boxing shown on their frescoes. The Mycenaeans adopted Minoan games and also raced chariots in religious or funerary ceremonies.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer")'s heroes participate in athletic competitions to honor the dead. In the *Iliad* there are chariot races, boxing, wrestling, a foot race, as well as fencing, archery, and spear throwing. The *Odyssey* adds to these a long jump and discus throw.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Pok_ta_pok_ballgame_maya_indians_mexico_3.JPG/250px-Pok_ta_pok_ballgame_maya_indians_mexico_3.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pok_ta_pok_ballgame_maya_indians_mexico_3.JPG)

The ball in front of the goal during a game of pok-ta-pok, 2006

It was in [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") that sports were first instituted formally, with the first [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") recorded in 776 BCE in [Olympia](/wiki/Olympia,_Greece "Olympia, Greece"), where they were celebrated until 393 CE.[[22]](#cite_note-22) These [ancient Olympic Games](/wiki/Ancient_Olympic_Games "Ancient Olympic Games") consisted of [running](/wiki/Running "Running"), [long jump](/wiki/Long_jump "Long jump"), [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling"), [Pankration](/wiki/Pankration "Pankration") ([combat sport](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport")), [discus throw](/wiki/Discus_throw "Discus throw"), and [javelin throw](/wiki/Javelin_throw "Javelin throw").[[23]](#cite_note-23) In the [Bayankhongor Province](/wiki/Bayankhongor_Province "Bayankhongor Province") of [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia"), Neolithic-era cave paintings dating to 7000 BC depict a wrestling match surrounded by crowds.[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Prehistoric](/wiki/Prehistoric "Prehistoric") cave-paintings in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") show a sport similar to [sumo](/wiki/Sumo "Sumo") wrestling.[[25]](#cite_note-Crego2003-25) In Wadi Sura, near [Gilf Kebir](/wiki/Gilf_Kebir "Gilf Kebir") in [Libya](/wiki/Libya "Libya"), a [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") rock painting in the [cave of swimmers](/wiki/Cave_of_swimmers "Cave of swimmers") shows evidence of [swimming](/wiki/Human_swimming "Human swimming") and [archery](/wiki/Archery "Archery") being practiced around 6000 BC.[[26]](#cite_note-Vörös2007-26)

Team sports have a rich and ancient history dating back thousands of years. These activities served as important facets of society, not only for physical fitness but also for social, cultural, and even political purposes. In ancient civilizations, team sports were prevalent and often intertwined with religious and cultural practices. In [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica"), the [Aztec](/wiki/Aztec "Aztec") ball game, [ollamaliztli](/wiki/Mesoamerican_ballgame "Mesoamerican ballgame"), was not just a sport but also a ritual with symbolic significance.[[27]](#cite_note-Mesoamerican-27)

The [Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), who laid the foundations for many contemporary sports, held various team sports as central to their culture. The Olympic Games, first recorded in 776 BCE, featured events like chariot racing and team foot races, fostering unity and friendly competition among city-states.[[28]](#cite_note-Ancient_Greek-28) The [Spartans](/wiki/Spartans "Spartans"), known for their military prowess, engaged in team sports like the [episkyros](/wiki/Episkyros "Episkyros"), a type of football.[[29]](#cite_note-Ancient_Greek_Spartans-29)

[Rome](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") adopted and adapted many Greek sports, introducing [harpastum](/wiki/Harpastum "Harpastum"), a ball game similar to soccer, and [ludi circenses](/wiki/Ludi#Ludi_circenses "Ludi"), which included team chariot racing. These sports provided a sense of entertainment and unity, while also serving as a means of social control.[[30]](#cite_note-Rome-30)

In [ancient China](/wiki/History_of_China#Ancient_China "History of China"), [cuju](/wiki/Cuju "Cuju") was a popular team sport akin to modern soccer, played as early as the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") (206 BCE – 220 CE). The sport was not only a form of entertainment but also a means of fostering camaraderie among communities.[[31]](#cite_note-fifa-or-31)

Throughout history, team sports have reflected the values and priorities of their respective cultures. Whether it was the competitive spirit of the Greeks, the discipline of the Romans, or the communal bonding in China, ancient team sports played an integral role in the social fabric of civilizations. They transcended mere physical activity, serving as a testament to the enduring significance of sports in human history. Today, these ancient traditions continue to influence modern team sports, reminding us of the timeless appeal and cultural importance of collective athletic endeavors.
